14.summary: The product has built out an impressive head-to-head comparison infrastructure — nine dedicated compare pages covering Larafast, SaaSyKit, Laravel Spark, Jetstream, Wave, Makerkit, Shipfast, SupaStarter, and NextjsSaas — plus an index. The inline comparison table on the landing page benchmarks against Breeze, Jetstream, Filament Starter, and SaaSyKit across six dimensions. Key differentiators are clearly articulated: Redis-locked billing, full TypeScript React admin panel, 90+ tests, and 11 feature flags. Gaps remain: pricing vs. competitors is not surfaced on compare pages, the Boilerplate.dev and SaasRock competitors are absent, and the comparison data lacks last-verified timestamps rendered visibly to visitors (the field exists in props but is not prominently displayed).

findings.title: Strong compare-page coverage but two significant competitors are absent
findings.description: The /compare directory covers 9 alternatives but omits Boilerplate.dev (a popular Laravel SaaS starter) and SaasRock (Laravel + React, direct stack match). Both are commonly discovered in the same search queries as this product. Missing them leaves SEO and conversion gaps for visitors who have evaluated those specific tools.

evidence.note: Lists Larafast, SaaSyKit, Wave, Laravel Spark, Makerkit, Shipfast, SupaStarter, NextjsSaas, Jetstream — no Boilerplate.dev or SaasRock
No Boilerplate.tsx or SaasRock.tsx file exists

recommendations.title: Add /compare/boilerplate and /compare/saasrock pages
recommendations.details: Both are direct Laravel competitors discovered via the same organic searches. Each page should follow the existing ComparisonTable pattern with verified feature rows. Focus differentiators: Redis-locked billing, TypeScript React stack, full test suite coverage.
recommendations.success_metric: Two new compare pages indexed within 30 days; combined organic impressions >500/mo within 90 days

findings.title: Compare pages omit competitor pricing, reducing conversion impact
findings.description: The individual compare pages (e.g., LaravelSpark.tsx, Larafast.tsx) use a ComparisonTable component focused on features but do not surface a direct pricing comparison row. Visitors evaluating cost cannot quickly see that this product's one-time purchase model differs fundamentally from Spark's subscription or Larafast's one-time tiers. This is a high-leverage conversion signal being left unused.

evidence.note: ComparisonTable driven by server-side features prop — no pricing row visible in component interface
Index card shows 'price' field ('One-time') but individual compare pages do not replicate this

recommendations.title: Add a pricing row to every individual compare page
recommendations.details: Include a 'Pricing model' row in each ComparisonTable showing one-time vs. subscription, approximate price points, and license terms. This directly addresses the purchase objection for buyers who arrived from a competitor search.
deliverables: artifact::updated comparison feature data in controllers/controllers serving ComparisonPageProps
recommendations.success_metric: Compare page conversion-to-pricing click rate increases measurably (track via ENGAGEMENT_CTA_CLICKED with source='compare_*')

findings.title: Landing page comparison table covers only free/scaffolding tools, not paid direct competitors
findings.description: The Welcome.tsx inline comparison benchmarks against Laravel Breeze, Jetstream, Filament Starter, and SaaSyKit — but Larafast, Shipfast, and Makerkit (all paid, direct-stack competitors) are absent from the home page table. A visitor who has evaluated Larafast before arriving has to navigate to /compare/larafast to see the head-to-head. The landing page comparison is the highest-visibility placement.

evidence.note: comparisonAlternatives = ['Laravel Breeze', 'Jetstream', 'Filament Starter', 'SaaSyKit'] — no Larafast, Makerkit, Shipfast

recommendations.title: Add a second comparison table row-set for paid competitors on the landing page
recommendations.details: Either expand the existing table or add a second table focused on paid alternatives (Larafast, Makerkit, Shipfast). Keep the free-tools table for the 'why not just use Breeze?' audience segment. This targets the mid-funnel buyer who is comparing paid options.
recommendations.success_metric: Time on landing page section increases; compare page bounce rate from home page decreases

findings.title: Unique differentiators are strong but the Redis billing story is undersold in copy
findings.description: The product's most technically defensible advantage — Redis-locked double-charge prevention — appears in the hero headline and comparison table but is not explained in a dedicated feature page or guide. Competitors do not offer this. Buyers who understand race conditions in billing will immediately recognize the value; buyers who don't will skip past it. The /features/billing page exists but the 'why this matters' narrative is thin.

evidence.note: Comparison row 'Billing (Redis-locked)' exists but no explanatory link
Feature page exists — content depth not assessed but the route is available
StripeBillingGuide.tsx exists — check whether it covers the race condition prevention story specifically

recommendations.title: Add an explainer section on the billing feature page about double-charge prevention
recommendations.details: Write 2-3 paragraphs explaining why concurrent Stripe API calls cause double charges, how Redis locks prevent this, and what the failure mode looks like without it. Link from the comparison table 'yes' cells. This converts a feature claim into a trust-building technical proof.
deliverables: artifact::blog post or guide on Redis billing locks
recommendations.success_metric: Features/Billing page exit rate decreases; time on page increases

15.summary: The product has a coherent voice anchored in concrete specificity — numbers (90+ tests, 11 flags, 14-day trial, 2-3 days to deploy) rather than vague claims. The hero headline leads with a defensible technical claim. Testimonials are present but are generic defaults (Alex M., Sarah K., James T.) with no company names, photos, or verifiable context, which limits trust transfer. The product name 'Laravel React Starter' is purely descriptive — it is a template name, not a product brand. Social proof signals default to '100+ developers' and are prop-driven, meaning they can be updated from the server, but the default values shown when the prop is not set are low. The pricing page meta description references 'Laravel React Starter' as the product name in a generic way. Brand identity is functional but not memorable.

findings.title: Default testimonials are clearly placeholder content — no names, photos, or company context
findings.description: The Welcome.tsx defines three DEFAULT_TESTIMONIALS with generic names (Alex M., Sarah K., James T.) and no company affiliations, profile photos, or Twitter handles. These are the values rendered when no server-side testimonials prop is provided. Placeholder testimonials signal to perceptive buyers that social proof has not been earned, undermining trust at the critical first-impression stage.

evidence.note: DEFAULT_TESTIMONIALS defined with no photos, no company names, no verifiable identity signals
testimonials = DEFAULT_TESTIMONIALS used as fallback — if server sends real testimonials, defaults are replaced

recommendations.title: Collect and display real, attributed testimonials before launch
recommendations.details: Reach out to beta users or early access buyers. Request a 1-2 sentence quote, their full name, role, company, and optionally a Twitter handle or GitHub link. Even 2 real testimonials outperform 10 placeholder ones. Ship the real quotes via the server-side prop so DEFAULT_TESTIMONIALS are never rendered in production.
recommendations.success_metric: All testimonials in production have full name + role + company; no generic 'Alex M.' pattern visible

findings.title: Social proof numbers default to low/round values that signal placeholder content
findings.description: The Welcome.tsx defaults userCount to 100, testCount to 90, and planCount to 4. The pricing page has 'Join hundreds of developers already building with Laravel React Starter.' These are credible but vague. The metrics bar on the landing page shows '100+ Developers using it' which is a standard placeholder-tier number. The githubStars prop is conditionally rendered only when provided, suggesting no public GitHub repo with stars exists yet.

evidence.note: featureCount=11, testCount=90, planCount=4, userCount=100 are defaults — real values come from server
'Join hundreds of developers' — round number, not specific
githubStars rendered only when !== undefined — implies no public stars signal currently configured

recommendations.title: Replace round-number social proof with specific, continuously updated figures
recommendations.details: Once real purchase data exists, update userCount to the actual license count (e.g., '47 developers' is more credible than '100+'). Consider adding a public GitHub repo to accumulate stars as a social proof signal. Track purchases in admin and expose the count to the WelcomeController.
deliverables: artifact::WelcomeController prop injection for real user count
recommendations.success_metric: Social proof numbers in production reflect actual license counts within ±10%

findings.title: Product name 'Laravel React Starter' is descriptive but not a memorable brand
findings.description: The product name is a category descriptor, which is strong for SEO but weak for brand recall. When a customer recommends it to a colleague, they say 'some Laravel React starter kit I found' rather than 'I used [BrandName]'. Competitors like Shipfast, Makerkit, and Wave have distinctive names that aid word-of-mouth. The pricing page title tag and meta description repeat the full generic name. The branding components (Logo, TextLogo) exist, suggesting a custom brand identity is partially in place.

evidence.note: appName = import.meta.env.VITE_APP_NAME || 'Laravel React Starter' — name is env-configurable but defaults to descriptor
Meta description: 'Start free, upgrade when you're ready. Simple per-seat pricing for Laravel React Starter' — generic
Logo and TextLogo components exist — custom brand assets are in place

recommendations.title: Establish a distinct brand name alongside the descriptive template name
recommendations.details: Consider a short, memorable product name (e.g., 'Foundry', 'Launchkit', 'Stackr') to use in social contexts while keeping 'Laravel React Starter Kit' as the SEO subtitle. The VITE_APP_NAME env var makes this a one-line change per deployment. Prioritize this for the marketing site; the actual template code can remain generic.
recommendations.success_metric: Product referred to by name in >50% of social mentions (not just 'that Laravel starter'

findings.title: Voice is consistent and technical-but-accessible, with one recurring placeholder pattern in email notifications
findings.description: The landing page, pricing page, comparison pages, and guide pages share a consistent voice: direct, specific, no hype adjectives, proof-anchored claims. Email notification copy (WelcomeSequenceNotification, TrialNudgeNotification, ReEngagementNotification) is competent but uses phrases like 'your existing tools' and 'smarter activity tracking and faster load times across the board' (hardcoded in ReEngagementNotification::accountStatus) that are clearly placeholder content — the latter is even more problematic as it appears in a win-back email as a concrete changelog claim.

evidence.note: Hardcoded: $changelogItem = 'smarter activity tracking and faster load times across the board' — this is placeholder copy shipped in the email body
Getting-started email tells users to 'Explore the features' with no product-specific context — appropriately generic for a template

recommendations.title: Replace hardcoded placeholder changelog claim in ReEngagementNotification
recommendations.details: The string 'smarter activity tracking and faster load times across the board' is a filler claim that will be sent verbatim to real users of any app built on this starter. This needs to either (a) be made configurable via config/app or a user-customizable string, or (b) removed and replaced with a link to the /changelog route which each deployer will populate. Do not ship placeholder marketing copy in transactional emails.
deliverables: artifact::ReEngagementNotification.php fix — configurable or dynamic changelog reference
recommendations.success_metric: No hardcoded product-specific claims in any notification class; all copy either dynamic or template-documented

16.summary: The template ships with an unusually strong content infrastructure for a starter kit: a blog with index and show pages, a guides section with 8 long-form guides (LaravelSaaS, StripeBilling, Webhooks, TwoFactor, TenancyArchitecture, FeatureFlags, BuildVsBuy, SaasStarterKitComparison), a changelog page, a roadmap page with voting, and 9 comparison pages. This is SEO-capable out of the box. What is absent: no email list capture mechanism on the landing page or pricing page (no newsletter signup, no waitlist form, no lead magnet), no Product Hunt asset checklist, no social media links in the footer, and no documented first-90-day launch plan. The announcement banner infrastructure exists (AnnouncementBanner component with props) but no campaign-ready copy.

findings.title: No email list capture on landing or pricing pages
findings.description: The landing page (Welcome.tsx) and pricing page (Pricing.tsx) have no newsletter signup, waitlist form, or lead magnet capture. The only email touchpoint is account registration. For a paid template product, a significant portion of visitors will want to 'follow for updates' before purchasing. Without capture, these visitors are permanently lost. The AnnouncementBanner component could include a capture CTA but currently has no form infrastructure.

evidence.note: No newsletter/waitlist form component anywhere in the file
No lead capture below pricing cards or in the footer section
AnnouncementBanner infrastructure exists and accepts props — could carry a capture CTA

recommendations.title: Add an email capture section to the landing page and pricing page
recommendations.details: Add a simple 'Stay updated — major releases, launch discounts, and tutorials' section with email input above the footer on both pages. Integrate with a simple ESP (Mailchimp, ConvertKit, Resend) via a new Contact-style form endpoint. This is the minimum viable launch list infrastructure.
recommendations.success_metric: Email capture form visible on / and /pricing; >5% of landing page visitors subscribe pre-launch

findings.title: Product Hunt launch readiness: infrastructure exists but assets are not prepared
findings.description: A Product Hunt launch requires a tagline, a 260-character description, a gallery of 5 screenshots, a first comment, and a maker bio. None of these are documented or prepared. The template does include an og-image.png reference in meta tags, which is a start. The roadmap page (Roadmap.tsx) and changelog page (Changelog.tsx) exist, which are useful PH launch assets. The Buy.tsx page suggests a one-time purchase flow exists, which is PH-compatible. The missing pieces are the actual launch assets and a documented launch-day playbook.

evidence.note: og:image, og:title, og:description, twitter:card all configured — meta assets ready
No LAUNCH_CHECKLIST.md or PH_ASSETS/ directory
Purchase flow page exists — PH launch can direct traffic here

recommendations.title: Prepare a Product Hunt launch asset kit
recommendations.details: Create a /docs/launch/ directory with: tagline (60 chars), description (260 chars), 5 screenshot descriptions, first comment draft, maker bio, hunter ask, and a launch-day timeline. Also prepare the og-image.png at 1200x630. Schedule for a Tuesday-Thursday launch window (highest PH traffic).
recommendations.success_metric: PH launch kit complete 2 weeks before target date; og-image.png renders correctly on PH preview

findings.title: Blog and guides infrastructure is excellent but no content exists yet
findings.description: The template ships with Blog/Index.tsx, Blog/Show.tsx, and a Guides section with 8 long-form guide pages (LaravelSaasGuide, StripeBillingGuide, WebhookGuide, TwoFactorGuide, TenancyArchitectureGuide, FeatureFlagsGuide, BuildVsBuyGuide, SaasStarterKitComparison). These are SEO-targeted pages built into the template itself. This is a significant content marketing asset — these pages, when populated with real content by the buyer, will drive organic traffic. However, since this is a starter template, the content on these guide pages is presumably placeholder or demo content. The content infrastructure is production-ready; the content calendar is absent.

evidence.note: 8 guide pages exist as routes in the template — SEO infrastructure ready
Blog infrastructure exists
No content calendar, no editorial plan for the template vendor's own marketing site

recommendations.title: Publish 3 cornerstone articles on the template vendor's own marketing site at launch
recommendations.details: The 8 guide routes are available. Populate at minimum: (1) 'How to Launch a Laravel SaaS in a Weekend' (already has LaravelSaasGuide.tsx route), (2) 'Laravel SaaS Starter Kit Comparison 2025' (SaasStarterKitComparison.tsx route), (3) 'Build vs. Buy: When to Use a SaaS Starter Kit' (BuildVsBuyGuide.tsx route). These three alone can drive significant organic traffic for high-intent buyer keywords.
deliverables: artifact::3 published guide articles (>1000 words each, real content not placeholder)
recommendations.success_metric: 3 guides indexed in Google within 30 days of publish; each targets a navigational or informational keyword with <50 KD

findings.title: No social proof strategy or community touchpoints in the footer
findings.description: The PublicFooter component exists but it is unknown whether it includes social media links (Twitter/X, GitHub, Discord). There is no documented community strategy (no Discord link, no Twitter account mentioned in copy). The pricing page references 'Reply to this email' in multiple notification templates but there is no community hub. For a developer-targeted product, a GitHub presence and a Twitter/X account are table-stakes launch channels.

evidence.note: Component exists — social link presence not confirmed without reading the file
No Discord, Twitter, or GitHub community links visible in hero or feature sections

recommendations.title: Establish and link social presence before launch
recommendations.details: At minimum: Twitter/X account for product updates, GitHub org for public demo repo (accumulates stars), and optionally a Discord for buyer support. Add footer links. A pre-launch tweet thread announcing the product with a waitlist link is standard for indie dev tool launches.
recommendations.success_metric: Footer contains at least 2 social/community links; Twitter account has >100 followers before PH launch

17.summary: This is the strongest area in the GTM audit. The product ships with a comprehensive email lifecycle infrastructure: WelcomeSequenceNotification (3 emails), OnboardingReminderNotification (3 emails), DunningReminderNotification (3 emails, day 3/7/12), TrialNudgeNotification (3 emails with dynamic days-left), TrialEndingNotification, WinBackNotification (3 emails, day 3/14/30), InvoluntaryChurnWinBackNotification, ReEngagementNotification (4 emails with paid/free branching and user score segmentation), UpgradeNudgeNotification (score-based), ExpansionNudgeNotification, LimitThresholdNotification, and PaymentFailedNotification. All notifications are queued, dual-channel (database + mail for verified users), and use HasUnsubscribeLink. Dedup logic uses the notifications table or EmailSendLog. The sequences are well-structured. Minor gaps: the ReEngagementNotification contains a hardcoded placeholder changelog claim (see BRAND-004), the WelcomeSequenceNotification email 2 subject is 'AppName: 3 things to try first' which is generic, and there is no milestone celebration email (e.g., on first API token creation, first project, first subscription).

findings.title: No milestone celebration emails for key activation moments
findings.description: The ActivationMilestone analytics event is defined (ACTIVATION_MILESTONE in AnalyticsEvent.php) and tracked from the frontend, but there is no corresponding MilestoneNotification email class. When a user creates their first API token, completes onboarding, or (critically) subscribes for the first time, they receive no celebratory email. These are high-leverage moments to reinforce the purchase decision and set expectations for the next step. The PaymentRecoveredNotification exists (for payment method updates), but no SubscriptionConfirmationNotification or 'You are now on Pro' email is visible.

evidence.note: ACTIVATION_MILESTONE event defined and tracked
No MilestoneCelebrationNotification.php or SubscriptionConfirmedNotification.php file
Payment recovered notification exists — subscription-started confirmation does not

recommendations.title: Add milestone emails for activation and subscription confirmation
recommendations.details: At minimum: (1) a 'You're now on Pro' email sent after SUBSCRIPTION_CREATED — confirms plan, features unlocked, next steps; (2) an 'Activation complete' email sent after ONBOARDING_COMPLETED event if billing is disabled. These reduce buyer's remorse and increase retention in the critical first 24 hours post-purchase.
deliverables: artifact::listener binding in EventServiceProvider or observer
recommendations.success_metric: 100% of new paid subscribers receive a plan-confirmation email within 5 minutes of subscribing

findings.title: Trial-to-paid sequence is comprehensive but the mid-trial nudge is underpowered
findings.description: TrialNudgeNotification email 1 (halfwayEmail) subject is 'AppName: N days left in your trial' — a countdown framing which creates mild urgency but does not surface value. The email body asks 'Have you explored the features that matter most to you?' — a question that prompts no specific action. The urgency email (email 2) is stronger. Research on trial conversion consistently shows that mid-trial emails perform best when they surface a specific high-value feature the user has NOT yet tried, rather than a generic check-in.

evidence.note: halfwayEmail body: 'Your trial gives you full access to everything — no features are held back.' — positive framing but no feature-specific CTA
Command exists and is feature-gated on billing.enabled

recommendations.title: Rewrite mid-trial (email 1) to surface one specific unactivated feature
recommendations.details: Check whether the user has created an API token, configured webhooks, or visited the admin panel. Send the mid-trial email with a specific 'You haven't tried X yet — here's why it matters' hook. This requires querying user activity in the command but dramatically improves trial-to-paid conversion. The ReEngagementNotification already implements a similar user-score-based branching pattern that can be adapted.
deliverables: artifact::SendTrialNudges command enrichment with activity check
recommendations.success_metric: Mid-trial email click-through rate increases vs. baseline; trial conversion rate improves >2% points

findings.title: Hardcoded placeholder changelog claim in ReEngagementNotification accountStatus email
findings.description: ReEngagementNotification::accountStatus() hardcodes the string 'smarter activity tracking and faster load times across the board' as a changelog claim. This is placeholder copy that will be sent verbatim to real users of any SaaS built on this starter, making a false product claim about improvements that may not exist. This is a correctness and trust issue, not just a brand issue.

evidence.note: $changelogItem = 'smarter activity tracking and faster load times across the board'; — hardcoded filler shipped in production email

recommendations.title: Replace hardcoded changelog claim with a configurable or dynamic value
recommendations.details: Options in order of preference: (1) Read the most recent public changelog entry from the database/config and use its title; (2) Replace with a link to the /changelog route with copy 'See what we've shipped since your last visit →'; (3) Remove the specific claim and replace with a generic 'We've been shipping — come see what's new' + changelog link. Do not ship this as-is.
recommendations.success_metric: No hardcoded product-specific claims in any notification; changelog reference is dynamic or route-linked

18.summary: The analytics infrastructure is among the strongest in this codebase. AnalyticsEvent.php defines 80+ events across auth, onboarding, trial, billing, admin, user actions, engagement, activation, errors, and PQL limit signals. The AnalyticsGateway sends to GA4 via Measurement Protocol with PII stripping, batch support, and a 25-event-per-request limit. DispatchAnalyticsEvent is a queued job with retry logic (2 tries, 10s/60s backoff). The Admin ProductAnalytics page surfaces signup trend (7d), onboarding funnel (start rate, completion rate), activation rate, feature adoption table, and subscription events. The limit threshold events (50/80/100%) serve as PQL signals. Key gaps: no explicit north star metric is defined anywhere in code or docs, the activation definition (onboarding_completed) is reasonable but not documented, the ProductAnalytics page covers only 30-day windows with no time range selector, and there is no retention cohort view or MRR trend in the admin analytics.

findings.title: No explicit north star metric defined — activation is tracked but not declared
findings.description: The ProductAnalytics page tracks activation_rate as 'activated_users / total_users who completed onboarding' but there is no config, comment, or documentation defining the north star metric for the template. The limit threshold events (LIMIT_THRESHOLD_50/80/100) are the strongest PQL signal but are not surfaced in the admin analytics dashboard — they appear only in GA4. An explicit north star + leading indicators definition helps the buyer understand what to optimize after deploying the template.

evidence.note: Activation defined as 'completed onboarding' — implicit definition, not documented
LIMIT_THRESHOLD_50/80/100 defined as PQL signals — not surfaced in admin ProductAnalytics page
No METRICS_FRAMEWORK.md or north star metric documentation

recommendations.title: Surface PQL limit-threshold events in the admin ProductAnalytics dashboard
recommendations.details: Add a 'PQL Signals' card to ProductAnalytics showing how many users hit 50%/80%/100% of their plan limits in the last 30 days. This is the most actionable upgrade trigger signal available and is already tracked — it just needs to be surfaced. Also add a brief doc comment in ProductAnalyticsController explaining the activation definition.
recommendations.success_metric: Admin can see weekly count of users hitting each limit threshold without querying GA4 directly

findings.title: Admin analytics has no MRR trend or revenue metrics
findings.description: The Admin Dashboard has a billing stats section (AdminBillingStatsService) but the ProductAnalytics page focuses on engagement and activation, not revenue. There is no MRR trend chart, no trial-to-paid conversion rate metric, and no churn rate calculation in the admin views. The subscription_events stat on ProductAnalytics shows counts of created/canceled/resumed but not the revenue impact. For a SaaS template targeting founders, these are the metrics they will care most about immediately after launch.

evidence.note: SubscriptionEvents type has created/canceled/resumed counts but no MRR or revenue fields
Billing stats service exists — likely feeds Admin/Dashboard.tsx billing section, not ProductAnalytics

recommendations.title: Add MRR trend and trial conversion rate to ProductAnalytics or Admin Dashboard
recommendations.details: Compute estimated MRR from active subscriptions × plan price (already in config/plans.php). Add a 30-day MRR trend chart and a trial-to-paid conversion rate (TRIAL_STARTED events vs TRIAL_CONVERTED events). These are the two metrics founders check daily. They can be approximate (server-computed from subscription table) without a Stripe API call.
deliverables: artifact::MRR trend data in ProductAnalyticsController or AdminBillingStatsService
recommendations.success_metric: Admin can see current MRR estimate and 30-day trial conversion rate on the analytics page

findings.title: Analytics events are comprehensive but have no time-window selector in the admin UI
findings.description: The ProductAnalytics page hardcodes '30 days' and '7 days' windows in the UI labels and presumably in the controller queries. There is no date range selector or comparison period. For a growing SaaS, founders need to compare week-over-week activation rates and see whether a product change improved funnel metrics. The AdminStatsGrid and AdminAreaChart components exist and support data passed as props — the missing piece is a time-range control in the controller and UI.

evidence.note: Static labels: 'last 30d', 'Signup Trend (7 days)', '30 days' — no period selector in component
No period prop or URL param for time range — fixed windows only

recommendations.title: Add a period selector (7d / 30d / 90d) to the ProductAnalytics page
recommendations.details: Pass a ?period= URL param to the ProductAnalytics controller, default to 30d. The AdminAreaChart already accepts data as props — only the controller query and the UI selector need to change. This is a low-effort, high-utility addition for buyers actively monitoring their metrics.
recommendations.success_metric: Admin can switch between 7d/30d/90d views without any fixed-window label being incorrect
